coredata.dc:title: Dual-analyte detection of Pb2+ and L-cysteine using a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juan
Chen, Qinqin
Meng, Xiangzhi
Wang, Donghan
Liu, Huiling
Feng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The increasing presence of heavy metals and amino acids in environmental and pharmaceutical samples necessitates the development of effective and sensitive detection methods. Traditional analytical techniques often lack the specificity and sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, leading to a need for novel approaches. In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on a physical mixture of red and blue carbon dots (R/BCDs) for the selective detection of lead ions (Pb2+) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). The probe was synthesized through hydrothermal methods and characterized by various spectroscopic techniques. The R/BCDs probe exhibited a remarkable 11.6-fold fluorescence quenching in response to Pb2+, with a detection limit of 15 nM, while subsequent addition of L-Cys showed a 9.6-fold restored fluorescence signal, achieving a detection limit of 75 nM. Additionally, we demonstrated the feasibility of fabricating colorimetric test paper and hydrogels, enabling rapid on-site analysis. This dual-analyte detection capability was demonstrated through successful application in real water and pharmaceutical samples. The R/BCDs probe offers a novel, simple, and effective strategy for environmental monitoring and biological assessments, highlighting its potential for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lead
L-Cysteine
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent test paper and hydrogel preparation
Structural characterization of RCDs and BCDs
Fluorescent paper and hydrogel for real sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the Joint Fund Project of the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (YDZJ202401554ZYTS). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (http://www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance with testing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of R/BCDs probes after adding different concentrations of Pb2+ (0–1.5 μM), (b) linear relationship between I681 /I450 and Pb2+ concentration, (c) the corresponding photographs under a 365 nm UV light, (d) fluorescence spectra of R/BCDs-Pb2+ after addition of different concentrations of L-Cys, (e) plot of I681 /I450 versus the L-Cys concentration, inset: the linear relationship between Log (I681 /I450) and L-Cys concentration (0.1 to 400 μM), (f) the corresponding fluorescence photos under a 365 nm UV light.

(a) Fluorescence response of R/BCDs to metal ions (a-c), anions (d), small molecules (e); Fluorescence response of R/BCDs-Pb2+ to anions (f) and small molecules (g), the fluorescence photos of R/BCDs-Pb2+ to small molecules under 365 nm UV lamp (h).

(a) The fluorescence decay curves of the R/BCDs solution after the addition of 0, 0.2, 0.6, 1.2 and 1.5 μM Pb2+ , (b) absorption spectrum of R/BCDs, R/BCDs with different concentrations of Pb2+ and R/BCDs-Pb2+ -L-Cys, (c) FT-IR spectra of R/BCDs and R/BCDs-Pb2+ , (e) Zeta potential of R/BCDs solution with different concentrations of Pb2+ and L-Cys.
Fluorescent test strips and hydrogels for detection of Pb2+ and L-Cys in actual samples.
Synthesis and applications of R/BCDs ratiometric fluorescence probe.
Detection of Pb2+ in real water samples.
Detection of L-Cys in capsule samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-analyte detection of Pb2+ and L-cysteine using a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jilin Provincial International Joint Research Center of Photo-functional Materials and Chemistry, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: R/BCDs ratiometric fluorescence probe were developed for sensitive quantitative and on-site visual detection of Pb2+ and L-cys.

The increasing presence of heavy metals and amino acids in environmental and pharmaceutical samples necessitates the development of effective and sensitive detection methods. Traditional analytical techniques often lack the specificity and sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, leading to a need for novel approaches. In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on a physical mixture of red and blue carbon dots (R/BCDs) for the selective detection of lead ions (Pb2+) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). The probe was synthesized through hydrothermal methods and characterized by various spectroscopic techniques. The R/BCDs probe exhibited a remarkable 11.6-fold fluorescence quenching in response to Pb2+, with a detection limit of 15 nM, while subsequent addition of L-Cys showed a 9.6-fold restored fluorescence signal, achieving a detection limit of 75 nM. Additionally, we demonstrated the feasibility of fabricating colorimetric test paper and hydrogels, enabling rapid on-site analysis. This dual-analyte detection capability was demonstrated through successful application in real water and pharmaceutical samples. The R/BCDs probe offers a novel, simple, and effective strategy for environmental monitoring and biological assessments, highlighting its potential for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead (Pb2+) is one of the most toxic metal ions, posing severe health risks and environmental pollution [1–4]. Its widespread presence in industrial activities such as battery production, pigments manufacturing, paper-making, ammunition, and coal burning often leads to significant contamination. Pb2+ contamination in water sources is particularly alarming, as its persistence and toxicity make it a critical environmental and public health issue. Lead exposure can cause a range of health problems, including anemia, neurological impairments, and irreversible damage to the cognitive and behavioral development of children and adolescents [3–5]. Conventional detection methods for Pb2+ include voltammetry [6], atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], atomic emission spectroscopy [8], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9]. While these methods are highly sensitive and accurate, they are typically limited to laboratory settings. The requirement for sophisticated instruments, extensive sample pretreatment and trained personnel makes them unsuitable for rapid and on-site analysis, which is essential for timely monitoring and environmental protection. Similarly, L-cysteine (L-Cys), as an essential amino acid, involves in various biological processes, including protein synthesis, detoxification, and metabolism [10–13]. Abnormal levels of L-Cys are associated with many health issues, such as skin disorders, liver damage, neurodegenerative diseases and hair depigmentation. Traditional detection techniques for L-Cys, including chromatography [14] and electrochemical methods [11,12], are often limited by their complexity, need for expensive equipment and lengthy sample pretreatment, which hinder their applicability in routine diagnostics and environmental monitoring [15,16]. Therefore, there is an increasing demand for simple, cost-effective, and accurate methods for the rapid, on-site detection of Pb2+ and L-Cys in real samples, particularly in medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring.

Fluorescent probes have become powerful tools for the sensitive detection of various analytes, including metal ions and biomolecules [17,18]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and ease of synthesis [19–22]. By functionalizing CDs with various surface groups, they can selectively interact with specific analytes, offering a versatile platform for fluorescence-based sensing. While many existing methods focus on single-analyte detection, the dual-sensing of Pb2+ and L-Cys presents additional challenges due to the need for high sensitivity and specificity in detecting both analytes simultaneously in complex samples. In particular, traditional optical sensors often suffer from low selectivity, requiring highly controlled conditions, which limits their practical applications. These disadvantages highlight the need for novel sensing platforms that can provide reliable, multiplexed detection in practical, on-site settings [5,23].

Recent advances in ratiometric fluorescence probes, which utilize the fluorescence intensity ratios of two distinct fluorophores, have shown significant promise in improving the sensitivity and specificity of detection [22,24]. For example, Liu et al. [25] developed a dual-color CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence test strip for semi-quantitative detection of Cu2+ with a detection limit as low as 25 nM. Zhan et al. used a ratiometric probe combining cyan CDs and a ruthenium complex for detecting nitrite ions (NO2 −), which changed color from cyan to red with increasing NO2 − concentration. Yu et al. [20] constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based probe using gold nanoclusters and CDs for detecting cysteine. These approaches address the limitations posed by variations in experimental conditions and instrumental performance, as the fluorescence ratio remains stable under varying conditions [25–28]. The use of such ratiometric probes offers distinct advantages, including wide color changes, which make them ideal for portable fluorescence devices, such as test strips and hydrogels, facilitating on-site detection in field settings [28–30]. However, despite the progress in this area, there have been no reports on CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and L-Cys.

In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe by combining red and blue CDs (RCDs and BCDs) to achieve rapid visual detection of Pb2+ and L-Cys with high sensitivity and selectivity (Scheme 1 ). Pb2+ quenched the red fluorescence of RCDs without affecting the blue fluorescence of BCDs, resulting in a visible color change from pink to blue under UV light. Subsequently, the addition of L-Cys restored the quenched red fluorescence, causing the color to revert from blue to pink, enabling the detection of L-Cys. This dual-analyte detection approach not only enhances the specificity and sensitivity for both analytes but also facilitates easy on-site visual identification. We further extended the applicability of this probe by fabricating fluorescent test papers and hydrogels, which were successfully used for on-site visual detection of Pb2+ in water samples and L-Cys in capsules, showing high reliability and practicality. This study aims to advance the development of simple, efficient, and highly sensitive sensors for environmental and health monitoring, with a focus on real-time, on-site applications. The dual-analyte detection system offers a promising solution to address key public health and environmental challenges, paving the way for more accessible and practical detection technologies.

Glutathione and formamide were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Trimethylol aminomethane was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Other reagents and chemicals were purchased from Tianjin Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory. Water used throughout the study was doubly distilled water (>18 MΩ cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission election microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Shimadzu XRD-6100 spectrometer. Surface chemical features were obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 360 FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer). Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu RF-5301 spectrophotometer. UV − vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer.

BCDs were prepared via a facile hydrothermal method according to our previous work [31]. Briefly, 0.02 mol of oxalic acid dihydrate and 0.02 mol of anhydrous ethylenediamine were dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water. After ultrasound treatment for 5 min, the mixture was heated at 200°C for 3 h.

The RCDs were synthesized following previously reported methods with some modifications [32]. Initially, 0.68 g of reduced glutathione (GSH) was dissolved in a beaker containing 20 mL of formamide under ultrasonic treatment. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a reaction vessel and heated to 160 °C for 4 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for one week, with the external dialysis medium replaced every 12 h. After dialysis, the solution within the bag was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter, yielding a deep green RCDs solution. Finally, the prepared RCDs were stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

To explore the fluorescence quenching effect of Pb2+ on R/BCDs, 40 μL of BCDs solution, 35 μL of RCDs solution and 100 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7, 0.01 M) were mixed in a 5 mL centrifuge tube. After full mixing, different concentrations of Pb2+ were injected and diluted with deionized water to 5 mL. After incubation for 6 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the emission wavelengths were 450 and 681 nm, and the excitation and emission slits were 5 nm and 3 nm, respectively).

Similarly, the fluorescence recovery effect of L-Cys on the R/BCDs-Pb2+ system was explored as follows. Different concentrations of L-Cys solutions were added into a 5 mL centrifuge tube containing 40 μL of BCDs, 35 μL of RCDs, 100 μM Pb2+ and 100 μL of Tris-HCl. The mixtures were diluted with deionized water. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the emission wavelength were 450 and 681 nm, and the excitation and emission slits were 5 nm and 3 nm, respectively).

The actual samples used for Pb2+ detection included surface water, laboratory tap water, and river water sourced from Majiagou. Real water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter, and then the concentration of Pb2+ was measured using both ICP-MS and the proposed method. To further assess the recovery, we added various standard Pb2+ solutions to the real water samples, followed by filtration. The proposed method and ICP-MS were then used to test the samples, and the spiked recoveries were obtained by calculation.

For the detection of L-Cys, different cysteine capsules were used for testing. The exact content of cysteine in the capsules is provided in the product instructions. To ensure the analysis concentration falls within the effective working range of the ratiometric fluorescence probe, the drug in the capsules was dissolved in water and appropriately diluted to prepare standard solutions. The accurate concentration of the solution was calculated based on the dilution factor. The proposed method was then used to test the samples, and the results were compared with the calculated concentration to evaluate the accuracy and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test paper and hydrogel preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of fluorescent test paper: the fiber filter paper was cut into small strips of the same size, and then the strips were immersed in the mixed RCDs: BCDs fluorescence intensity ratio of 5:1 solution. The fluorescent test paper was then obtained by natural drying away from light.

Preparation of fluorescent hydrogels: fluorescent hydrogels were prepared using the methods described in the literature [33]. Sodium alginate (1 g) was added to deionized water (70 mL), then the mixture was heated and stirred at 80 °C to form a viscous solution, and the bubbles were removed by ultrasound. Then sodium alginate solution (14.3 mg/mL, 10 mL) and mixed CDs solution (fluorescence intensity ratio: 5:1) were added to CaCl2 solution (100 mM, 5 mL) to rapidly form CDs hydrogel microspheres within 1 min.

CDs typically exhibit excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility, making them highly suitable for environmental and biological sensing applications. Their high resistance to photobleaching contributes to the prolonged retention of fluorescence signal. Additionally, CDs are easy to synthesize, cost-effective and scalable, which render them advantageous materials for practical, large-scale applications. Herein, we aim to design two types of CDs with red and blue fluorescence emissions (RCDs and BCDs), respectively, to construct a fluorescence ratiometric probe for the detection of Pb2+ and L-Cys. The combination of red and blue fluorescence provides a broad color evolution range, facilitating naked-eye detection under UV light, which is particularly useful for portable, on-site detection applications [29]. The ability to measure the ratio between two distinct fluorescence emissions helps eliminate the influence of external factors (such as instrumental fluctuations or environmental changes), ensuring more reliable and accurate readings [24,30].

The BCDs were prepared according to our previous method [31], while the RCDs were prepared following previously reported methods with some modifications [32]. Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence response was used as the reference for optimizing the synthesis conditions. As shown in Fig. S1 , the optimal conditions for the synthesis of BCDs were a precursor ration of 1:1, a reaction temperature of 200 °C and a reaction time of 3 h, yielding a QY of 25.89 %. We then investigated the fluorescence responses of BCDs synthesized under different precursor ratios to Pb2+ and L-Cys. It was found that BCDs synthesized with varying N doping levels showed negligible fluorescence responses to the detection, indicating that BCDs can serve as a reference signal in the ratiometric fluorescence probe. For the synthesis of RCDs, we used GSH as the sole carbon precursor and employed a hydrothermal method in formamide solution. The higher N and S doping effectively enhanced the QY and promoted a red shift in the emission wavelength. As shown in Fig. S2 , the RCDs obtained by reacting at 160 °C for 4 h exhibited the highest QY of 8.32 %. Similarly, we examined the fluorescence responses of RCDs to Pb2+ and L-Cys under various synthesis conditions. It was observed that with increasing reaction temperature, the sensitivity to Pb2+ decreased, likely due to excessive carbonization at higher temperatures, which may have resulted in the loss of binding groups on the surface. Within a reaction time range of 2–4 h, no significant difference in fluorescence sensitivity was observed. Therefore, based on both the QY and response characteristics, the optimal synthesis conditions were determined to be a reaction at 160 °C for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of RCDs and BCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained BCDs are spherically dispersed with various functional groups on their surface ( Fig. S3 ). The structure and composition of RCDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR spectrum and XPS spectra. As depicted in Fig. S4
a, the TEM images reveal that the synthesized RCDs are quasi-spherical in shape and exhibit excellent dispersion. A statistical analysis of more than 200 RCDs fitted with a Gaussian function is presented in Fig. S4
b, indicating a size distribution range of 1.0–6.0 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 3.17 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) clearly revealed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.32 nm (see the inset of Fig. S4
a). The surface functional groups of the RCDs were identified by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. S4
c, a broad peak in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of –OH and –NH2 groups [34]. Characteristic peaks for the amide bonds appear at 1683 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and C-N, respectively [35]. The absorption peak at 1608 cm−1 is associated with bending vibrations of CO/CN [36]. Additionally, absorption peaks at 1000–1100 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 are attributed to CS, oxidized S, and N-H groups. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the RCDs ( Fig. S4
d) shows a broad peak at 2θ = 24.3°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of graphite, indicating an amorphous carbon structure [37].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to characterize the surface elemental composition and states of RCDs. As illustrated in Fig. S5
a, RCDs are composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S), with corresponding peaks observed at 285, 402, 532, and 169 eV, and atomic percentages of 23.3 %, 9.9 %, 51.1 %, and 15.7 %, respectively. This suggests that the surface of RCDs may be passivated by functional groups containing N, O, and S, which facilitates long-wavelength emission. The high-resolution C1s spectrum reveals the presence of CC/C-C (284.9 eV), C-N/C-O (286.5 eV), and CO (288.8 eV) ( Fig. S5
b) [38]. The N1s high-resolution spectrum displays a component corresponding to N-H bond (402.0 eV) ( Fig. S5
c). The O1s high-resolution spectrum is deconvoluted into two binding energies at 532.2 eV and 533.2 eV, ascribed to C-O and CO, respectively ( Fig. S5
d). The high-resolution S2p spectrum exhibited two fitting peaks at 169.1 eV and 170.3 eV, attributed to the presence of -C-SO3-C- and -C-SO4-C- ( Fig. S5
e) [39]. Notably, the FTIR characteristics were consistent well with the findings from the XPS analysis.

The
Fig. S6 displays the fluorescence emission spectra of BCDs, RCDs, and R/BCDs under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The emission peaks of the R/BCDs probe are consistent with those of the individual BCDs and RCDs, appearing at approximately 450 nm and 681 nm, respectively. As illustrated in the inset, under 365 nm UV light, the BCDs, RCDs, and R/BCDs emit blue, red, and pink fluorescence, respectively.

To establish the optimal conditions for Pb2+ detection, we refined key experimental parameters, including the fluorescence intensity ratio of R/BCDs, pH value, and reaction time. Pb2+ solutions ranging from 0 to 1 μM were introduced into both the BCDs and RCDs, and the changes in fluorescence intensity were monitored. As shown in Fig. S7
a, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs remained relatively stable with increasing Pb2+ concentration, whereas that of RCDs continuously decreased. However, under UV illumination, the color change in fluorescence was minimal, and was limited primarily to variations in brightness, making visual differentiation challenging (see the inset of Fig. S7
b).

As depicted in Fig. S8 , varying the mixing ratios of RCDs and BCDs resulted in distinct fluorescence color changes during Pb2+ detection. The fluorescence intensity ratios (I681/I450) of 3:1, 5:1, and 7:1 were tested against different concentrations of Pb2+ (0 ∼ 0.9 μM), and their corresponding color transitions were observed in the insets of Fig. S8 . At an I681/I450 ratio of 3:1, the R/BCDs fluorescence probe transitioned from light pink to gray-blue and ultimately to blue ( Fig. S8
a). In contrast, at a 7:1 ratio, the fluorescence probe exhibited a color shift from red to light purple, resulting in blue gray ( Fig. S8
c). When the ratio was set at 5:1, the R/BCDs fluorescence probe displayed an evolution from pink to purple, blue-gray, and finally to blue ( Fig. S8
b). Consequently, we selected the fluorescence intensity ratio of 5:1 for BCDs to RCDs as the optimal ratio to achieve the broadest and most continuous color evolution.

To accurately detect Pb2+ and L-Cys, we subsequently investigated the influence of solution pH values on the fluorescence response for Pb2+ detection. As illustrated in Fig. S9
a, the quenching effect reached its maximum at pH 8. Next, we exposed the R/BCDs fluorescence probe to 365 nm UV light for 80 min to evaluate its photostability. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I681/I450) showed negligible changes, confirming excellent fluorescence stability ( Fig. S9
b). Furthermore, we studied the effect of reaction time on the detection of both Pb2+ and L-Cys. As shown in Fig. S9
c, the I681/I450 ratio gradually decreased with time after the addition of 1.5 μM Pb2+ and stabilized at approximately 5 min. In R/BCDs- Pb2+ solution, the addition of 2 mM L-Cys stabilized after approximately 3 min. Therefore, 6 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for Pb2+ detection, and 5 min was selected for L-Cys detection.

Under the optimized detection conditions, Fig. 1
a shows the fluorescence response spectra of R/BCDs to various concentrations of Pb2+ and the corresponding I681/I450 values. As the Pb2+ concentration increased from 0 to 1.5 μM, the fluorescence intensity at 681 nm gradually decreased, while the intensity at 450 nm remained relatively constant. Consequently, the I681/I450 ratio gradually decreased with increasing Pb2+ concentration. A good linear relationship was observed between the I681/I450 values and Pb2+ concentrations within the range of 0–1.4 μM (Fig. 1 b), with the linear regression equation being as follows:
I681/I450=4.8549–3.2087CPb 2+

The correlation coefficient R2 was found to be 0.9987. LOD was calculated based on 3σ/KSV, where σ is the standard deviation of nine blank solution’s fluorescence signal, and the Ksv is the slope of the linear equation [24,29,30]. The LOD was calculated to be 15 nM. The fold fluorescence quenching at 1.4 μM Pb2 was calculated to be 11.6. Additionally, the spectral response to Pb2+ caused a gradual color transition under 365 nm UV light, evolving from pink to purple, then to gray-blue, and finally to blue (Fig. 1 c). This sensitive spectral response and color evolution suggest a novel strategy for visual detection of Pb2+.

The composition of real samples is often complex, making the selectivity of fluorescent probes a critical indicator for their application in real sample detection. To evaluate the selectivity for Pb2+ detection, we investigated various metal ions, anions, and small molecules under identical conditions. As shown in Fig. 2
a-d, only Pb2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence of the R/BCDs probe solution, with a quenching ratio of approximately 90 % for 1.50 μM Pb2+. Other potential interfering ions and small molecules had minimal effects on the fluorescence of R/BCDs. Additionally, the change in fluorescence color could be directly observed with naked eye under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2 c). This finding indicates that the prepared R/BCDs ratiometric fluorescence probe solution exhibits exceptional selectivity for Pb2+.

As shown in Fig. 1 d, as the concentration of L-Cys increased from 0 to 2000 μM, the fluorescence that was quenched by Pb2+ could be effectively restored to approximately 90 % of the original intensity. There was a progressive recovery in the fluorescence signal after the addition of 400 μM L-Cys (9.6 fold). A good linear correlation exists between log(I681/I450) and logCL-cys, when the L-Cys concentration ranges from 0.1 μM to 400 μM (Fig. 1 d and e), with the following linear fitting equation:
log
(
I 681
/
I 450
)
= 0.3028 log
C
L - c y s
- 0.1203

The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9945, allowing the detection limit for L-Cys to be calculated as 75 nM. Additionally, under 365 nm UV light, the color transitions from blue to blue-gray, then to purple, and finally to pink as the concentration of L-Cys increases (Fig. 1 f).

To validate the selectivity of the R/BCDs- Pb2+ system towards L-Cys, we also tested its fluorescence response to various anions and other small molecules. As shown in Fig. 2 f-h, in addition to L-Cys, the addition of 11 different anions and 14 other small molecules resulted in negligible changes in I681/I450. This clearly demonstrates the exceptional selectivity of the R/BCDs- Pb2+ system for L-Cys.

As shown in Fig. 3
a, the fluorescence lifetime of R/BCDs varied minimally with increasing Pb2+ concentration, changing from 4.48 ns to 4.31 ns, indicating the presence of static quenching in the quenching process [29,40,41]. Standard Stern-Volmer equation was also employed for further investigate the possible quenching mechanism ( Fig. S10 ). The observed upward curvature in the relationship between the I0/I ratio at 681 nm and Pb2+ concentration suggests that the interaction between Pb2+ and RCDs became more complex at higher concentrations, leading to a stronger quenching effect (Fig. 3 b). Specifically, at low Pb2+ concentrations, the Ksv (1.28 × 106 M−1) was much greater than the limiting diffusion rate constant, which indicated the quenching could probably belong to static quenching. As the Pb2+ concentration increased, static quenching effects might arise, where Pb2+ interacts with CDs, possibly through coordination or non-fluorescence complex formation, leading to a more intense quenching effect and a non-linear increasing trend [41,42]. The UV absorption spectra (Fig. 3 b) revealed that as the Pb2+ concentration increased, there was a significant decrease in absorption ranging of 350–450 nm, a new absorption peak appeared at approximately 474 nm, and the absorption at 680 nm increased, suggesting the formation of a new complex.

The FT-IR spectra of R/BCDs and R/BCDs-Pb2+ (Fig. 3 c) demonstrated significant changes in the N-H/O-H stretching vibrations between 3000–3500 cm−1, a weakening of the C = O characteristic peak at 1683 cm−1, the disappearance of the C = O/C = N peak at 1608 cm−1, a notable increase in the C-N stretching vibration at 1397 cm−1, and the disappearance of the C = N absorption peak at 1307 cm−1, alongside the appearance of a new peak at 835 cm−1. These changes indicate the formation of a complex between Pb2+ and R/BCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3 d, the zeta potentials for various concentrations of Pb2+ (0, 0.2, 0.6, 1.2, and 1.5 μM) were −5.32, −1.87, −1.15, −0.61, and 0.04 eV, respectively, indicating an increase in potential with increasing Pb2+ concentration. Upon gradual addition of L-Cys at 1, 10, and 100 μM, the potential decreased to −1.61, −3.25, and −15.16 eV, respectively. This suggests that the adsorption of Pb2+ onto the surface of R/BCDs results in the formation of a complex that leads to fluorescence quenching, whereas L-Cys, which has a stronger affinity for Pb2+, is capable of restoring the fluorescence of R/BCDs. Hence, the quenching mechanism is inferred to be static quenching.

Upon the addition of L-Cys (green curve in Fig. 3b), the weakened absorption at 350–450 nm and 628 nm was restored, the new peak at 474 nm disappeared, and the absorption at 628 nm was enhanced again, indicating that L-Cys has a stronger affinity for Pb2+, which preferentially forms a complex with L-Cys. It is speculated that L-Cys is an amino acid with a thiol group, which plays a crucial role in the formation of strong interaction with Pb2+, leading to a relaxation of the quenching effect. Other amino acids without thiol groups (such as glycine, alanine or serine) do not exhibit this type of interaction, which accounts for the high selectivity toward L-Cys.
The applicability of the R/BCDs ratiometric fluorescence probe was then evaluated in practical applications for Pb2+ and L-Cys detection in real samples.

As shown in Table 1 , no Pb2+ was found in real water samples (tap water, surface water and river water) by our proposed method and ICP-MS. The recoveries obtained from the spiking experiment was ranging from 97.99 % to 103.02 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.08 % to 2.82 %, demonstrating good reliability and accuracy.

In addition, for L-Cys detection, we used Cys capsules as the real samples. The results obtained by the R/BCDs-Pb2+ system were consistent with the accurate content of Cys stated in the capsule's instructions. Satisfactory recoveries and RSDs in Table 2 further validated the reliability and feasibility of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent paper and hydrogel for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated the applicability of fluorescent paper and hydrogels for detecting Pb2+ in real water samples. By directly observing the color changes of the fluorescent paper and hydrogel under UV light, we tested their ability to detect Pb2+ in real samples. The color change trends of the paper and hydrogel upon the addition of different concentrations of Pb2+ were similar to those observed with standard solutions (Fig. 4 ). Notably, the Pb2+ standard color reference card demonstrates that each 0.2 μM incremental dose can be clearly distinguished by naked eye. This indicates that both the fluorescent paper and hydrogel exhibit excellent visual effectiveness and accuracy, making them highly reliable for rapid on-site detection of Pb2+ in real water samples.

Similarly, we explored the capability of fluorescent paper and hydrogel to detect L-Cys in capsules. When different concentrations of L-Cys capsule solutions were analyzed, the color change trends were in good agreement with those of standard solutions (Fig. 4). Therefore, fluorescent paper and hydrogels can effectively facilitate visual detection of L-Cys in capsules. Compared with CDs aqueous solutions, CDs-based paper and hydrogels are easier to use and operate, significantly simplifying the detection process and presenting broader application prospects.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on R/BCDs was successfully established utilizing red and blue CDs to detect Pb2+ and L-Cys. Through optimization of the detection conditions, a fluorescence intensity ratio of 5:1 was achieved, facilitating a broad range of color changes. The developed methods demonstrated high sensitivity, with detection limits of 15 nM for Pb2+ (11.6-fold) and 75 nM for L-Cys (9.6-fold), accompanied with visual identification under UV light. Furthermore, the creation of fluorescent paper and hydrogels highlights the practical applicability of the proposed probe, increasing the ease of use and field deployment for environmental monitoring and pharmaceutical analysis. Overall, these findings highlight the potential of R/BCDs as versatile tools for the rapid and effective detection of hazardous substances.
Consent to participate was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Consent for publication was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Juan Hou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qinqin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiangzhi Meng: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Donghan Wang: Data curation. Huiling Liu: Data curation. Wei Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
The article does not contain any experiments with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the Joint Fund Project of the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (YDZJ202401554ZYTS). The authors also would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (http://www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance with testing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112576.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
